title: A Ballad Of Marjorie
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½What ails you that you look so pale,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  O fisher of the sea?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½ן¿½Tis for a mournful tale I own,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Fair maiden Marjorie.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½What is the dreary tale to tell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  O toiler of the sea?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½I cast my net into the waves,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Sweet maiden Marjorie.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½I cast my net into the tide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Before I made for home;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Too heavy for my hands to raise,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  I drew it through the foam.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½What saw you that you look so pale,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Sad searcher of the sea?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½A dead manן¿½s body from the deep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  My haul had brought to me!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½And was he young, and was he fair?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½Oh, cruel to behold!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  In his white face the joy of life
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Not yet was grown a-cold.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½Oh, pale you are, and full of prayer
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  For one who sails the sea.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½Because the dead looked up and spoke,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Poor maiden Marjorie.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½What said he, that you seem so sad,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  O fisher of the sea?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  (Alack! I know it was my love,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Who fain would speak to me!)ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½He said, ן¿½Beware a womanן¿½s mouth-
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  A rose that bears a thorn.ן¿½ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½Ah, me! these lips shall smile no more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  That gave my lover scorn.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½He said, ן¿½Beware a womanן¿½s eyes.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  They pierce you with their death.ן¿½ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½Then falling tears shall make them blind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  That robbed my dear of breath.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½He said, ן¿½Beware a womanן¿½s hair-
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  A serpentן¿½s coil of gold.ן¿½ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½Then will I shear the cruel locks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  That crushed him in their fold.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½He said, ן¿½Beware a womanן¿½s heart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  As you would shun the reef.ן¿½ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½So let it break within my breast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  And perish of my grief.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½He raised his hands; a womanן¿½s name
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Thrice bitterly he cried:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  My net had parted with the strain;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  He vanished in the tide.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½A womanן¿½s name!ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What name but mine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  O fisher of the sea?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  ן¿½A womanן¿½s name, but not your name,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Poor maiden Marjorie.ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Dora Sigerson Shorter represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Dora Sigerson Shorter's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
